coredata.dc:title: Double-enhanced fluorescence of graphene quantum dots from cane molasses via metal and PEG modification for detecting metal ions and pigments
coredata.dc:creator: Lou, Ying
Sun, Wei
Jiang, Li
Fan, Xingang
Zhang, Kaiyou
Chen, Shuoping
Li, Ziyuan
Ji, Jianying
Ou, Jun
Liao, Lei
Qin, Aimiao
coredata.dc:description: Lead-doped graphene quantum dots modified with polyethylene glycol (PEG-Pb-GQDs) were one-step synthesized using biomass cane molasses as a green carbon source through simple hydrothermal method. The quantum yield (QY) of the PEG-Pb-GQDs can be as high as 30.31%, which is approximately three times that of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) without any modified, indicating that the doping of lead ions and the modification of polyethylene glycol (PEG) can realize the double-sided fluorescence enhancement effect. The as-prepared PEG-Pb-GQDs also exhibit stronger fluorescence, better stability and dispersion compared with lead doped graphene quantum dots (Pb-GQDs). The quasi-spherical PEG-Pb-GQDs were characterized by Fourier transforms infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to prove the success of lead ions (Pb2+) doping and PEG passivation modification. Multiple metal ions including Fe3+, Cu2+, Ag+, Co2+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Mn2+ and benzene sulfonic acid pigments including lemon yellow, quinoline yellow and sunset yellow can be detected by PEG-Pb-GQDs. Among the seven responsive metal ions, Fe3+ can be selectively detected under the condition of EDTA and thiourea as masking agent with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.29 μM for PEG-Pb-GQDs. Correspondingly, Cu2+ and Ag + can also be selectively detected by PEG-Pb-GQDs under appropriate masking agents. The benzene sulfonic acid pigments including lemon yellow, quinoline yellow and sunset yellow can also be selectively detected and the LOD were 0.067, 0.073 and 0.097 μM, respectively. The quenching mechanisms were dynamic quenching for Fe3+, static quenching for Cu2+ and Ag+ and static quenching and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) for pigments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PEG-Pb-GQDs
Cane molasses
Fluorescence double enhancement
Metal ions and pigments detection
Multiple quenching mechanisms

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of benzene sulfonic acid pigments
Selective detection of Fe3+、Cu2+ and Ag+
Fluorescence quenching mechanism by metal ions
Selective detection of pigments for PEG-Pb-GQDs
Sensitivity detection of pigments for PEG-Pb-GQDs

Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51564009 , 51468011 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi province (No. 2018JJA160029 ), the Foundation of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Optical and Electronic Materials and Devices ( 20AA-17 ), Guangxi Graduate Education Innovation Project ( YCB22021062 , YCSW22021199 ), Research Foundation of Guilin University of Technology (No. GUTQDJJ201901 ) and the Science and Technology Base and Talent Special Project of Guangxi Province (No. AD21220029 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of PEG-Pb-GQDs (inset: size distribution of the PEG-Pb-GQDs); (b) HRTEM image of PEG-Pb-GQDs (inset: enlarged view of HRTEM); (c) FTIR spectra of Pb-GQDs, PEG and PEG-Pb-GQDs; (d) XPS survey-scan spectrum of PEG-Pb-GQDs; High-resolution XPS spectra of (e) carbon, (f) oxygen and (g) lead of PEG-Pb-GQDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of Pb-GQDs and PEG-Pb-GQDs; (b) fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs, PEG-GQDs, Pb-GQDs and PEG-Pb-GQDs; (c) The photos of GQDs, PEG-GQDs, Pb-GQDs and PEG-Pb-GQDs under ultraviolet light (the bottom photos) and visible light (the above photos) from left to right, respectively; (d) Schematic illustration of fluorescence double enhancement mechanism for PEG-Pb-GQDs; (e) the stability of GQDs and PEG-GQDs for 35 days; (f) Zeta potential of Pb-GQDs and PEG-Pb-GQDs.

(a) Fluorescence responses of Pb-GQDs and PEG-Pb-GQDs to the different metal ions in absence of any masking agent; (b) the variation in relatively fluorescence intensity of Pb-GQDs and PEG-Pb-GQDs for detecting Fe3+ with various detection time; (c) calibration curves of the degree of fluorescence quenching [(F0–F)/F0 ] of PEG-Pb-GQDs versus Fe3+ concentration of Fe3+ alone solution; (d) Fluorescence responses of PEG-Pb-GQDs to the different metal ions in the presence of EDTA and thiourea; (e) calibration curves of the degree of fluorescence quenching [(F0–F)/F0 ] of PEG-Pb-GQDs versus Fe3+ ions concentration in the presence of EDTA and thiourea in mixed metal ions solution.

(a) Fluorescence responses of Pb-GQDs and PEG-Pb-GQDs to the common food additives including lemon yellow; (b) the variation in relatively fluorescence intensity of Pb-GQDs and PEG-Pb-GQDs for detecting lemon yellow with various detection time; (c) calibration curves of the degree of fluorescence quenching [(F0–F)/F0 ] of Pb-GQDs and PEG-Pb-GQDs versus the concentrations of lemon yellow alone solution and in the mixed food additive solution; (d) the photos of pigments and PEG-Pb-GQDs with pigments under ultraviolet light (the above photos) and visible light (the bottom photos). From left to right are lemon yellow, quinoline yellow, sunset yellow, PEG-Pb-GQDs and PEG-Pb-GQDs after adding lemon yellow, quinoline yellow, sunset yellow, respectively.

(a) Stern-Volmer curve for fluorescence quenching of PEG-Pb-GQDs by Fe3+ at different temperatures; (b) the UV–vis absorption spectra of PEG-Pb-GQDs in the presence of Fe3+ (black curve) and the superposition of the UV–vis absorption spectra of PEG-Pb-GQDs and Fe3+ solutions (red curve); (c) fluorescence decay of PEG-Pb-GQDs in the presence and abresence of Fe3+; (d) Stern-Volmer curve for fluorescence quenching of PEG-Pb-GQDs by lemon yellow at different temperatures; (e) overlap of fluorescence emission spectra of PEG-Pb-GQDs and UV–Vis absorption spectra of lemon yellow; (f) fluorescence decay of PEG-Pb-GQDs in the presence and abresence of lemon yellow; (g) quenching mechanism of PEG-Pb-GQDs for detection of Fe3+, Cu2+, Ag+ and pigments and energy level diagrams of corresponding quenching mechanisms.
Schematic of the synthesis of Pb-GQDs and PEG-Pb-GQDs from cane molasses.

head.ce:title: Double-enhanced fluorescence of graphene quantum dots from cane molasses via metal and PEG modification for detecting metal ions and pigments

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab New Processing Technology for Nonferrous Metals & Materials Ministry of Education, College of Materials Science and Engineering, College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab New Processing Technology for Nonferrous Metals & Materials Ministry of Education
College of Materials Science and Engineering
College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Lab New Processing Technology for Nonferrous Metals & Materials Ministry of Education, College of Materials Science and Engineering, College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center on Nanoenergy Research, College of Physical Science and Technology, Guangxi University, Nanning, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center on Nanoenergy Research, College of Physical Science and Technology, Guangxi University, Nanning, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lead-doped graphene quantum dots modified with polyethylene glycol (PEG-Pb-GQDs) were one-step synthesized using biomass cane molasses as a green carbon source through simple hydrothermal method. The quantum yield (QY) of the PEG-Pb-GQDs can be as high as 30.31%, which is approximately three times that of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) without any modified, indicating that the doping of lead ions and the modification of polyethylene glycol (PEG) can realize the double-sided fluorescence enhancement effect. The as-prepared PEG-Pb-GQDs also exhibit stronger fluorescence, better stability and dispersion compared with lead doped graphene quantum dots (Pb-GQDs). The quasi-spherical PEG-Pb-GQDs were characterized by Fourier transforms infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to prove the success of lead ions (Pb2+) doping and PEG passivation modification. Multiple metal ions including Fe3+, Cu2+, Ag+, Co2+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Mn2+ and benzene sulfonic acid pigments including lemon yellow, quinoline yellow and sunset yellow can be detected by PEG-Pb-GQDs. Among the seven responsive metal ions, Fe3+ can be selectively detected under the condition of EDTA and thiourea as masking agent with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.29 μM for PEG-Pb-GQDs. Correspondingly, Cu2+ and Ag + can also be selectively detected by PEG-Pb-GQDs under appropriate masking agents. The benzene sulfonic acid pigments including lemon yellow, quinoline yellow and sunset yellow can also be selectively detected and the LOD were 0.067, 0.073 and 0.097 μM, respectively. The quenching mechanisms were dynamic quenching for Fe3+, static quenching for Cu2+ and Ag+ and static quenching and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) for pigments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have become a research hotspot as a novel quasi-zero-dimensional fluorescent nanomaterial, which is thinner than carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and possess the property of more tunable band gap than other traditional two-dimensional luminescent materials [1]. GQDs have been applied in electrocatalysis [2], photocatalysis [3], nanomedicine [4], bioimaging [5], biosensing [6] and chemical sensing (such as metal ions including Al3+ [7], Fe3+ [8], Cu2+ [9], Ag+ [10], and Pb2+ [11] and non-metal molecules including hydrogen peroxide [12], ceftazidime [13], melamine [14], glucose [15], carbamate pesticides [16], colistin [17] and curcumin [18]) due to their superior physical and chemical properties. In the past few years, significant progress has been made in the preparation of GQDs. However, some researchers have paid more attention to improving the performance of GQDs while ignoring the environmental pollution caused by certain chemical reagents. Inspired by this, many natural biomass has been selected as green carbon source to prepare GQDs, including custard apple peel [19], pelagic Sargassum [20], alkali lignin [21], sugarcane bagasse [22] and rice husks [23], etc. Among them, cane molasses is a polyhydric mixture with a sugar content of 48.0%, which richness in multiple functional groups offers greater possibilities for detection applications. Unlike small molecules carbon source, biomass can not precisely control the content of a certain component. Therefore, the QY is slightly inferior to that of pure quantum dots. Metal doping and surface modification are the most common and effective ways to improve the QY of GQDs [24–26]. Wang et al. [27] used durian as carbon source to synthesize GQDs doped with sulfur atoms, and the QY can be as high as 79%. Zhang et al. [28] used a facile hydrothermal approach to generate boron-doped GQDs, which had been applied to the detection of glucose with a 0.03 μM limit of detection (LOD). Li et al. [29] used a microwave irradiation approach to synthesize GQDs modified with NaBH4, showing twice the QY compared with the GQDs without any treatment. Shen et al. [30] proposed to passivate and modify GQDs with PEG, the QY can reach 28%. Therefore, metal doping and surface modification provide a greater possibility for the large-scale production of high-quality GQDs.

Metal ions are an crucial part of maintaining the osmotic balance of the multiphase system. Their content is also an important indicator of whether the body is healthy. Iron ions (Fe3+) are indispensable substance for transmitting oxygen and participating in human metabolic activities. The balance will be broken when the content of Fe3+ is too much or too little, leading to the decrease of immune function and the susceptibility to bacterial infection [31]. The biochemical function of copper ions (Cu2+) for body is mainly catalysis. Lack of Cu2+ will reduce the activity of important enzymes in vivo and cause osteoporosis, while excessive Cu2+ will cause liver cirrhosis and even poisoning [32]. The same is true for other metal ions. Therefore, it is urgent to realize the convenient, accurate and fast detection of metal ions concentration method. At present, common methods of detecting metal ions include inductively coupled plasma method [33] and colorimetry method [34], but the expensive instruments and complex operation severely restrict the development of the former, and the latter is subject to great experimental error in detecting ions. Pigments are often added to food to improve its sensory properties. However, not only do they have no nutritional value, but excessive pigment content can also affect the physical function [35]. Some countries such as Norway also completely prohibit the use of any chemical synthetic pigments. Therefore, the detection of pigments in food is also bound to be put on the agenda. Niu et al. [36] designed an electrochemiluminescence sensor to detect sunset yellow in food. Lemon yellow from soft drink can be detected using GSH-CdTe QDs and the static quenching also be present by Li et al. [37] However, little relevant literature has been reported on the simultaneous detection of various ions or small molecules based only one material. Since the content of metal ions and pigments is an important indicator that threatens human health and food safety. Thence, it is very essential to develop a multifunctional GQDs for the simultaneous and rapid detection of multiple metal ions and pigments.

In this work, lead-doped GQDs modified with PEG were one-step synthesized using biomass cane molasses as a green carbon source through simple hydrothermal method, which possess stronger fluorescence, higher QY, better stability and dispersion in comparison with Pb-GQDs (Scheme 1 ). In addition to the sensitive detection of multi metal ions such as Cu2+, Ag+, Co2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Mn2+ and Fe3+ by PEG-Pb-GQDs, Fe3+ in mixed metal ions solutions can be selectively detected based on dynamic quenching under the masking of EDTA and thiourea, while the selective detection of Cu2+ was achieved by masking other interfering ions with F−, SCN− and Cl−, Ag+ under the masking agents of EDTA and F−, which were based on static quenching. The as-synthesized PEG-Pb-GQDs also were firstly applied to the detection of benzene sulfonic acid pigments including lemon yellow, quinoline yellow and sunset yellow based on the combined quenching mechanisms of static quenching and FRET.
The details of the materials and characterization are described in Supporting information S1 and S2, respectively. The synthesis of PEG-Pb-GQDs and Pb-GQDs re given in Supporting information S3.

The detection of metal ions was performed in pH = 7 with an excitation wavelength of 380 nm at room temperature. Taking Fe3+ as an example, 100 μL of Pb-GQDs stock solution was firstly diluted to 2 mL with PEG-200, and then different concentrations of Fe3+ ions (200 μL) were added to the above solution before fixing the volume to 5 mL with PEG-200 to study sensibility of the PEG-Pb-GQDs to metal ions. The selectivity study was carried out with various ions including Cr3+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Ni2+ with a concentration of 40 μM. The detection process was suitable to the Pb-GQDs and the detection of other metal ions (Cu2+, Ag+, Co2+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Mn2+). See Supporting information S4 and S5 for other experimental procedures and results for the detection of Fe3+ and pigments in real sample, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of benzene sulfonic acid pigments
ce:section.ce:para: The detection conditions for pigments are the same as above for metal ions. Taking lemon yellow as an example, the sensibility study of the PEG-Pb-GQDs was performed using fluorescent photometer to detect different concentrations (2–36 μM) of lemon yellow. The selectivity study the PEG-Pb-GQDs was carried out with various food additives including d-fructose (C6H12O6), glutamic acid (C5H9NO4), sucrose (C12H22O11), sodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7), ascorbic acid (C6H8O6), glucose (C6H12O6), thiourea (CH4N2S), cysteine (C3H7NO2S), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), β-cyclodextrin (C42H70O35), sodium benzoate (C7H5NaO2). The difference with PEG-Pb-GQDs for lemon yellow detection is that the solvent used in the detection process of Pb-GQDs is ultra pure water instead of PEG-200. The selective detection process of PEG-Pb-GQDs and Pb-GQDs to quinoline yellow and sunset yellow are similar to that of lemon yellow.

The TEM images of PEG-Pb-GQDs and Pb-GQDs are showed in Fig. 1 a and Supporting information S6, respectively. Both PEG-Pb-GQDs and Pb-GQDs are of quasi-spherical shape, but the dispersion of the former is obviously better than that of the latter. The size distribution histograms of Pb-GQDs and PEG-Pb-GQDs obtained by random counting of 100 particles are shown in the inset of Fig. 1a and Supporting information S6, respectively. The average particle size of Pb-GQDs and PEG-Pb-GQDs is 1.1 nm and 1.4 nm, respectively. The average size of PEG-Pb-GQDs and Pb-GQDs is almost the same, which could be attributed to the fact that PEG-Pb-GQDs and Pb-GQDs came from the stock solution with the same nucleation rate and the modification of PEG did not influence internal growth structure of PEG-Pb-GQDs. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of Pb-GQDs and PEG-Pb-GQDs in Fig. 1b and Supporting information S6 show that the lattice spacing of both samples was 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) lattice plane of graphite. The clear lattice fringes suggest that the prepared Pb-GQDs and PEG-Pb-GQDs have a highly uniform crystallinity [38].

The FT-IR spectra were collected to explore functional groups and composition of PEG-Pb-GQDs and Pb-GQDs on the surface. As can be seen in Fig. 1c, the broad absorption peaks at 3441 and 3381 cm−1 were due to the stretching vibrations of O–H. The peaks at 1640 and 1625 cm−1 demonstrate the existence of skeletal vibrations of CC [39]. The bonds of O–H and CC exist in both PEG-Pb-GQDs and Pb-GQDs. Furthermore, compared to the FTIR spectra of Pb-GQDs, the presence of peaks at 2893 and 1950 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibrations of C–H and the stretching vibrations of –CCC- for PEG-Pb-GQDs, respectively. The characteristic bands at approximately 1096 cm−1 could be assigned to the stretching vibration of C–O, indicating that PEG is successfully linked to the surface of PEG-Pb-GQDs [40].

Moreover, the compositions of the PEG-Pb-GQDs and Pb-GQDs were also investigated by XPS analysis. As can be seen from the survey XPS spectra in Fig. 1d, the PEG-Pb-GQDs contain carbon, oxygen elements and trace amounts of lead element, which are assigned to C 1s, O 1s and Pb4f respectively. The C 1s spectrum of PEG-Pb-GQDs in Fig. 1e shows three peaks at 284.3, 284.8, 285.9 and 288.6 eV which can be ascribed to the C–C/C–H, CC,C-OH and CO, respectively [41]. As shown in Fig. 1f, the O 1s peak could be deconvoluted into a peak at 532.2 eV, which corresponds to O–H [40]. The content of C–O–H in PEG-Pb-GQDs can be increased from 19.25% of Pb-GQDs to 39.98% after PEG passivation according to the XPS characterization in Fig. 1e and Supporting information S6, indicating that PEG successfully passivation of Pb-GQDs. In addition, the deconvoluted weak peak of Pb 4f spectrum shown in Fig. 1g at 138.8 eV is the evidence for the successful lead doping into the product [42] and the atomic ratio of lead was measured to be 0.3%. Thus, the doping of lead ions and the modification of PEG have been successfully achieved according to the results of FTIR and XPS.

To further study the optical properties of PEG-Pb-GQDs and Pb-GQDs, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were collected. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the obvious absorption peak at 260 nm existed in the UV–vis absorption spectra for both PEG-Pb-GQDs and Pb-GQDs, which is attributed to π−π* transition of the CC bond [43]. Compared with Pb-GQDs, the peak for PEG-Pb-GQDs is more sharper, which is due to the polarity of PEG is weaker than that of water as previously reported [44].

The prepared PEG-Pb-GQDs and Pb-GQDs in our experiment have a greater improvement in fluorescence performance than the GQDs previously reported by our research group [44]. As shown in Fig. 2b, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs without any heteroatom doping and modification is the lowest, and the fluorescence intensity of Pb-GQDs is almost the same as that of PEG-GQDs, which can reach 1.3 times as much as the fluorescence intensity of GQDs. The PEG-Pb-GQDs possessed the highest fluorescence intensity, which is nearly 1.8 times higher than GQDs. The photos of the samples under ultraviolet light and visible light are shown in Fig. 2c. The QY of Pb-GQDs is 13.17%, while that of PEG-Pb-GQDs can reach up to 30.31%, the QY of PEG-GQDs and GQDs is 21.32% and 10.44%, respectively. This result is equivalent to more than five times the C-dots (QY = 5.8%) reported previously with cane molasses [45]. The fluorescence enhancement of PEG-Pb-GQDs can be attributed to two reasons: on one hand, the doping of Pb2+ leads to the changes of Homo and Lumo energy levels and promotes electron transfer; on the other hand, the passivation of hydroxyl-rich PEG makes the photoexcited electrons of π-π* transition to sp [2] energy level, thereby enhancing the fluorescence performance of GQDs. In addition, the doping of Pb2+ and the passivation modification of PEG caused the red shift of the maximum emission peak position from 440 nm to 450 nm compared with GQDs, which is because the long-wavelength emission is related to the smaller size of GQDs after Pb2+ doping and the rich oxygen functional groups from PEG on the surface of PEG-Pb-GQDs according to the quantum confinement effect. The mechanism of fluorescence double enhancement for PEG-Pb-GQDs is shown in Fig. 2d. The fluorescence intensities of Pb-GQDs and PEG-Pb-GQDs first increase and then decrease with different excitation wavelengths from 280 nm to 500 nm according to Supporting information S7, and the maximal excitation wavelength for both PEG-Pb-GQDs and Pb-GQDs is 380 nm. The shift of the maximal emission peaks with the excitation wavelengths indicates the excitation wavelength dependence. Moreover, the change of fluorescence intensity of PEG-Pb-GQDs and Pb-GQDs every other week for 35 consecutive days was investigated as shown in Fig. 2e, the results display PEG-Pb-GQDs are more stable than Pb-GQDs. To further verify the accuracy of the results, it can be seen from Fig. 2f that the absolute potential value of PEG-Pb-GQDs with two different charges is significantly greater than that of Pb-GQDs with only one negative charge, which indicates that the stability of PEG-Pb-GQDs is indeed better than that of Pb-GQDs.

To investigate the response of different metal ions to PEG-Pb-GQDs and Pb-GQDs, various metal ions solutions (400 μM) were studied, including Cr3+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Mg2+ and Ni2+. It can be seen from Fig. 3 a that the synthesized PEG-Pb-GQDs in this study can not only improve the detection sensitivity of Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Mn2+, but also achieve the detection of Ag+ compared with the PEG-GQDs previously reported by our group [44]. It is speculated that the introduction of Pb2+ may affect the electronic energy level structure and the number of surface groups, thus inducing the response to Ag+. Among these responsive metal ions, the quenching degree of Fe3+ on the PEG-Pb-GQDs and Pb-GQDs is greater than that of other metal ions. In addition, the response effect of the PEG-Pb-GQDs by any metal ions is more sensitive than that of Pb-GQDs. The formation of hydrogen bonds averages the electron cloud density, thereby reducing the frequency of stretching vibrations, and the absorption peaks of proton donors shifts significantly to lower wavenumbers. The content of hydrogen bonds in PEG-Pb-GQDs increases significantly after PEG passivation from FT-IR in Fig. 1d. The interaction between Pb-GQDs and PEG promotes the dispersion of PEG-Pb-GQDs due to the presence of hydrogen bonds. Therefore, more detection sites that interact with ions were exposed and lower the LOD of detection target. As shown in Fig. 3b, the fluorescence quenching of both PEG-Pb-GQDs and Pb-GQDs by Fe3+ can be accomplished within 10 s. The fluorescence intensity decreases with the increase of Fe3+ concentration for Pb-GQDs from 0 to 210 μM and for PEG-Pb-GQDs from 0 to 150 μM as shown in Supporting information S8. It can be seen that (F0–F)/F0 (F and F0 refer to the fluorescence intensities in the presence and absence of metal ions, respectively) increases with the concentration of Fe3+ for Pb-GQDs, which presents a good linear relationship in the range of 120–210 μM with the R2 and the LOD of 0.9920 and 1.21 μM, respectively. The PEG-Pb-GQDs show a good linear relationship in the range of 12–52 μM and their R2 and LOD are 0.9980 and 0.32 μM respectively (Fig. 3c), which is more sensitive than the previously reported PEG-GQDs (15.00 μM) [44] and other reports (13.68 μM) [46]. Furthermore, the LOD of PEG-Pb-GQDs for detecting Cu2+, Ag+, Co2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Mn2+ is 0.37, 0.54, 0.57, 0.88, 0.93 and 0.97 μM, respectively. The calculation of the LODs and the linear relationship of (F0–F)/F0 with concentration of Cu2+, Ag+, Co2+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Mn2+ for PEG-Pb-GQDs are shown in Supporting information S9 and Table S1. The PEG-Pb-GQDs have great potential for detecting multiple metal ions in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective detection of Fe3+、Cu2+ and Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: Metal ions play an important role in regulating the metabolism of the human body. Fe3+ participates in the metabolic immunity and the formation of various functional enzymes in vivo. Cu2+ have a catalytic effect on the body. Ag+ also has a certain effect on wound healing, but excessive Ag+ can poison the human body. So it is significant to realize the qualitative and quantitative detection of metal ions. In general, the composition of the actual solution is complex and contains multifarious metal ions, while the relatively good response to the above seven metal ions were exhibited for both PEG-Pb-GQDs and Pb-GQDs. According to previous reports of our team, five metal ions including the Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, and Pb2+ can be successfully masked by EDTA [44]. It can be seen from Fig. 3d that EDTA has only a slight masking effect on Ag+ for PEG-Pb-GQDs. But thiourea can provide a good masking to Ag+ for PEG-Pb-GQDs. Therefore, the selective detection of Fe3+ was successfully achieved under the combined action of EDTA and thiourea by PEG-Pb-GQDs. A good linear relationship was presented in the range of Fe3+ concentration from 28 to 44 μM in multiple metal ions solution in Fig. 3e for PEG-Pb-GQDs and the LOD was 0.29 μM, which is similar to those obtained by detecting alone Fe3+ solution. Moreover, the selective detection of Fe3+ by Pb-GQDs in the above multiple metal ions can also be realized by the same method and the LOD was 1.21 μM (See Supporting information S10 for the detection details). These results showed that the existence of EDTA and thiourea can not only realize the selective detection of Fe3+, but also does not affect the sensitivity of PEG-Pb-GQDs and Pb-GQDs by Fe3+. The comparison of carbon dots applied in detection of Fe3+ has shown in Table S2. In addition, the selective detection of Cu2+ can be realized under the masking agents of F−, SCN− and Cl−. Similarly, Ag+ can be selectively detected under the masking agents of EDTA and F−. The detail can be seen in Supporting information S11. The LOD of Cu2+ and Ag + by PEG-Pb-GQDs was 1.08 μM and 3.24 μM. The selective detection of Co2+, Ni2+, Mn2+ and Pb2+ by PEG-Pb-GQDs is under study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism by metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The quenching mechanism of PEG-Pb-GQDs by metal ions continues to be studied inspired by the previous report of our team from the three aspects of quenching constant, UV–visible absorption spectrum and fluorescence lifetime to determine whether the quenching process is dynamic or static [44]. Take Fe3+ as an example, it can be seen from Fig. 5a that the slope of the linear fitting equation when the detection system is 40 °C is significantly greater than that of the detection system at 25 °C, which is one of the sufficient conditions for judging the dynamic quenching. Furthermore, the sum of the UV–visible absorption of PEG-Pb-GQDs and Fe3+ is consistent with that of the PEG-Pb-GQDs after adding Fe3+ as shown in Fig. 5b. However, the fluorescence lifetime of PEG-Pb-GQDs was reduced from 4.73 to 1.77 ns after adding of Fe3+ in Table S3 and Fig. 5c. Therefore, these results completely meet the criteria of dynamic quenching for PEG-Pb-GQDs. In addition, the fluorescence quenching mechanisms of PEG-Pb-GQDs by Cu2+ and Ag+ were also discussed, respectively. It can be seen from the results in Supporting information S12 and Table S3 that the quenching mechanism of PEG-Pb-GQDs by Cu2+ and Ag+ is just opposite to that of Fe3+, which belongs to static quenching. The quenching mechanisms of PEG-Pb-GQDs for detection of metal ions were shown in Fig. 5g. In this paper, the dynamic quenching is that the excited state molecules of PEG-Pb-GQDs collide with Fe3+ and lose their excitation energy through energy transfer or charge transfer, resulting in fluorescence quenching. The static quenching is the interaction between Cu2+ and Ag+ and the ground state molecules of PEG-Pb-GQDs to form new complexes. The negative charge on the surface of PEG-Pb-GQDs facilitates the interaction with cations Fe3+, Cu2+ and Ag+. According to related reports [47], the quenching mechanism between metal ions and fluorescent materials largely depends on the atomic environment, including the number of neighboring atoms, bond angles and local electronic states. The tendency of different metal ions to accept electrons from PEG-Pb-GQDs and the difference in affinity between them lead to different quenching mechanisms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective detection of pigments for PEG-Pb-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Lemon yellow and sunset yellow are commonly used synthetic food colorants. Excessive use of them in food may cause adverse health effects, including allergic reactions, migraines, and DNA damage. Quinoline yellow is even banned in food in China. Therefore, efficient and appropriate analytical techniques are needed to ensure health and food safety. Taking lemon yellow as an example, the detection method of pigments is similar to that of metal ions as mentioned above with PEG-Pb-GQDs and Pb-GQDs. But the selective detection of lemon yellow was studied with relatively common additives in food, including d-fructose, glutamic acid, sucrose, sodium citrate, ascorbic acid, glucose, thiourea, l-cysteine, hydrogen peroxide, cyclodextrin and sodium benzoate. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the two systems of both PEG-GQDs and PEG-Pb-GQDs have almost no response to other food additives except lemon yellow, which indicates that the selective detection of lemon yellow can be realized even in mixed solution of various food additives. And the quenching of both PEG-Pb-GQDs and Pb-GQDs can be realized by lemon yellow within 5 min in Fig. 4b. Since quinoline yellow and sunset yellow are similar to lemon yellow in structure, both of them have hydroxyl groups and sodium benzenesulfonate, the selective detection of quinoline yellow and sunset yellow were also carried out by both PEG-Pb-GQDs and Pb-GQDs (see in Supporting information S14).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity detection of pigments for PEG-Pb-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To further study the detection sensitivity of Pb-GQDs and PEG-Pb-GQDs to different synthetic pigments, the experiments were carried out to study the fluorescence intensity of the Pb-GQDs and PEG-Pb-GQDs changing with the concentration of pigments. The experimental results are shown in Supporting information S14. A good linear relationship is exhibited from 4 to 16 μM for lemon yellow concentration with the LOD of 0.090 μM for Pb-GQDs and from 4 to 20 μM for lemon yellow concentration with the LOD of 0.067 μM for PEG-Pb-GQDs (Fig. 4c). To achieve the accuracy of detection in real samples, the selective detection of lemon yellow was also conducted in the mixed solution of food additives (0.01 M). There was also a good linear relationship between the (F0–F)/F0 and the concentrations of lemon yellow with the LOD of 0.11 μM for Pb-GQDs and 0.081 μM for PEG-Pb-GQDs (Fig. 4c), which shows that the sensitivity detection of real samples can be realized completely. The comparison of various carbon dots applied in the detection of lemon yellow is shown in Table S2. The detection methods and procedures for quinoline yellow and sunset yellow were similar to the detection of lemon yellow using Pb-GQDs and PEG-Pb-GQDs. The details can be seen in Supporting information S13. Overall, the sensitivity of PEG-Pb-GQDs was higher than that of Pb-GQDs for the detection of the same pigment. The detection sensitivity of lemon yellow was the highest, followed by quinoline yellow, and sunset yellow was the lowest by both PEG-Pb-GQDs and Pb-GQDs. The photos of pigments and PEG-Pb-GQDs after adding pigments under ultraviolet light and visible light were shown in Fig. 4d.

The quenching mechanism of PEG-Pb-GQDs by pigments was also discussed from dynamic quenching and static quenching. Take lemon yellow as an example, it can be seen from Fig. 5d that the slope of the linear fitting equation decreases from 0.1249 to 0.1145 when the temperature of the quenching system rises from 25 °C to 40 °C. Moreover, the lifetime shown in Fig. 5f of the PEG-Pb-GQDs did not change significantly, which is in line with the standard of static quenching. But the result is just opposite to the quenching mechanism of PEG-Pb-GQDs by Fe3+. Similarly, static quenching was also one of the quenching mechanisms for the sensitivity detection of quinoline yellow and sunset yellow by PEG-Pb-GQDs. In addition, if the emission spectrum of donor overlaps with the absorption spectrum of acceptor, non-radiative energy transfer will occur, which will lead to fluorescence quenching of fluorescent materials according to the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) theory. As shown in Fig. 5e, the fluorescence spectrum of PEG-Pb-GQDs overlaps with the ultraviolet–visible spectrum of lemon yellow, which indicates that FRET was also the quenching mechanism. To further explore the accuracy of FRET, the energy transfer efficiency E between lemon yellow and PEG-Pb-GQDs was calculated as 0.5482 by FRET theoretical formula (see Supporting information S15), the distance between energy donor and acceptor was 1.08 nm, which fits the range of less than 8 nm for FRET. The quenching mechanism of PEG-Pb-GQDs for detection of pigments was shown in Fig. 5g. Furthermore, it can be seen from Table S4 that the shorter the distance between donor and acceptor, the greater the energy resonance transfer efficiency, which indicates that the greater the quenching degree of pigment to PEG-Pb-GQDs and the higher the sensitivity. The theoretical calculation results were consistent with the experimental results of sensitivity detection of PEG-Pb-GQDs by pigments mentioned above. In brief, the quenching of PEG-Pb-GQDs by the three different pigments was the results of a combination of static quenching and FRET (see Supporting information S15 for details). From the structure point of view, the common feature of the three pigments is that they all contain benzenesulfonic acid group. The surface of PEG-Pb-GQDs possesses a large amount of positive charges than Pb-GQDs, which can cause electrostatic interaction with the benzenesulfonic acid group from pigments. Moreover, many hydroxyl groups are concentrated on the surface of the PEG-Pb-GQDs, which can form hydrogen bonds with pigments, thus effectively shorten the distance between the PEG-Pb-GQDs and pigments and promote the occurrence of FRET.

In conclusion, blue fluorescent PEG-Pb-GQDs with a QY of 30.31% were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using sugarcane molasses as carbon source. The QY of PEG-Pb-GQDs was significantly increased by more than twice of Pb-GQDs (13.17%) and approximately three times higher than that of the previously reported GQDs (10.44%) without passivation, which indicates that the surface passivation of lead doping and PEG have double enhancement effects on the fluorescence performance of GQDs. The results of FTIR and XPS characterization showed that PEG-Pb-GQDs have been successfully modified by lead ions and PEG. The PEG-Pb-GQDs were applied to the sensitivity detection of seven metal ions, in which the selective detection of Fe3+ could be realized in the mixed metal ions solution under the masking of EDTA and thiourea. In addition, the mixed solution of F−, SCN− and Cl− can realize the masking of other metal ions in this paper except Cu2+. The selective detection of Ag + by PEG-Pb-GQDs can be achieved under the mask of EDTA and F−. In addition, benzene sulfonic acid pigments including lemon yellow, quinoline yellow, sunset yellow can be also detected by PEG-Pb-GQDs, which was applied the detection of lemon yellow in peppermint and sunset yellow in passion fruit drink. In addition, the quenching mechanism can be attributed to the dynamic quenching by Fe3+, static quenching by Cu2+ and Ag+, while the detection of pigments is the result of the combined effect of static quenching and FRET. The PEG-Pb-GQDs have higher sensitivity to metal ions and pigments than Pb-GQDs, indicating that PEG-Pb-GQDs are promising in practical analytical applications.

Aimiao Qin conceived and designed the study, and critically revised the manuscript. Ying Lou, Wei Sun performed the experiments, analyzed the data and drafted the manuscript with input from all authors. Xingang Fan and Jianying Ji participated in the experiments, data compilation and analysis. Lei Liao and Li Jiang participated in study design and manuscript revision. Kaiyou Zhang, Shuoping Chen, Ziyuan Li and Jun Ou supervised the experiment and provided resources. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript. 1These authors contributed equally, *Corresponding authors, **Corresponding authors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China(No.51564009, 51468011), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi province (No.2018JJA160029), the Foundation of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Optical and Electronic Materials and Devices (20AA-17), Guangxi Graduate Education Innovation Project (YCB22021062, YCSW22021199), Research Foundation of Guilin University of Technology (No.GUTQDJJ201901) and the Science and Technology Base and Talent Special Project of Guangxi Province (No.AD21220029).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2022.113037.
